aspects.name: Parallel lines cut by transversal
aspects.description: Apply angle relationships created when one (or more) transversals cross two parallel lines: corresponding, alternate interior/exterior, co-interior (same-side), and linear-pair relationships. The defining cue is the explicit assertion that two lines are parallel and a transversal intersects both.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 differs from A6 in that A1 always involves at least two parallel lines plus a transversal; A6 involves only intersecting lines (vertical angles) without parallelism. A1 differs from A2 in that the unknown is found through transversal angle rules, not the triangle angle-sum.

types.name: Solve for unknown angle (single transversal, parallels)
types.description: Stem shows two parallel lines crossed by one transversal; one angle is given numerically (or in a simple expression) and the student must report the measure of another labeled angle.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify which angle relationship (corresponding equal, alternate-interior equal, co-interior supplementary, vertical equal, linear pair) connects the given and target angles, then compute the missing measure.

characteristics: one known angle and one unknown angle
single transversal
relationship is corresponding, alternate-interior, or co-interior with a clearly labeled diagram
arithmetic limited to one subtraction (180 - g) or direct equality
may be a figure-only stem where the angle measure is shown in the diagram, not the prose
easy.example_stem: Lines m and n are parallel; transversal t makes a 134° angle at m. Find w at n.

must_preserve: explicit parallel statement
single transversal
exactly one given numeric angle and one unknown
must_vary: which relationship is used (corresponding vs alt-interior vs co-interior)
diagram orientation (top-left vs bottom-right of labeled angle)
numeric value (avoid 90° which trivializes)
common_bugs: agent computes 180 - g when the angles are actually equal (and vice versa)
agent forgets the figure-not-drawn-to-scale note and reasons from apparent size
agent treats the transversal as parallel to one of the lines

types.name: Algebraic angle equations from parallels
types.description: Two parallel lines and a transversal produce two angle measures expressed as linear expressions in a parameter (k, x); student must set them equal or supplementary and solve, then often report a derived quantity (e.g., x + 142, the other angle, or a value comparison).
types.core_skill_demand: Translate the geometric relationship (equal vs supplementary) into an equation in the parameter, solve for it, then plug back to compute the requested quantity.

characteristics: one angle numeric, one in form x or simple x + c
answer is x or x + given-number with no extra step
easy.example_stem: Lines p, q parallel; one angle is 142°, the other is x°. Find x + 142.

characteristics: both angles given as linear expressions in k (e.g., 6k+13 and 8k-29), or one expression set against a numeric angle
answer requires solving for k first then computing a different angle (e.g., z)
may include an inequality interpretation (which of the following must be true if y<65)
medium.example_stem: Lines m, n parallel; x = 6k+13, y = 8k-29. What is z?

must_preserve: parallel-lines configuration
linear expressions in one parameter
the requested quantity is derived (not just the parameter itself)
must_vary: which two angles are expression-bearing
coefficients/constants of the linear expressions
whether the relationship invoked is equality or supplementarity
common_bugs: agent sets equal when the geometry calls for supplementary (or vice versa)
agent solves for k and reports it as the final answer instead of plugging back
agent simplifies coefficients to trivial values that produce non-integer k

types.name: Multi-transversal / counting-relationships
types.description: Figure has two transversals (or four lines: two parallels + two transversals) so several labeled angles exist; stem asks which statement among several must be true, or to find an angle whose value depends on routing through multiple relationships.
types.core_skill_demand: Apply parallel-line and intersecting-line angle rules across two transversals, including angle-bisector or sum-of-angles substeps; eliminate distractor statements that are not forced by the geometry.

characteristics: two parallel lines, one transversal, with all four angles labeled; pick the correct relationship statement
easy.example_stem: Lines m, n parallel; line k a transversal forms 145° and x°. Which statement is true?

characteristics: four lines forming nested transversal/parallel configurations, possibly with an angle bisector
stem gives one acute or obtuse angle as a linear expression and asks for an algebraic sum of multiple angles (e.g., one acute + three obtuse)
answer requires identifying that the sum equals a fixed number of straight-angle worths (180°, 360°, 540°) regardless of x
hard.example_stem: A transversal cuts two parallel lines: one acute angle is (9x-560)°; sum of one acute + three obtuse = (-18x + w)°. Find w.

must_preserve: multi-transversal or angle-bisector configuration in the hard variant
answer depends on a symbolic identity (independent of the parameter)
must_vary: which angle is acute vs obtuse
coefficients in the linear expression
how many angles are being summed
common_bugs: agent collapses to a single-transversal problem and loses the parameter cancellation
agent counts the wrong number of acute vs obtuse angles in the sum
agent assumes the parameter has a specific value rather than treating it symbolically

aspects.name: Triangle angle sum / exterior angle
aspects.description: Apply the interior-angle sum (180°) of a triangle, the exterior-angle theorem, or both, to find an unknown angle. Typically two angle measures (or expressions for them) are supplied directly or implicitly via a linear-pair / vertical-angle / parallels relationship adjacent to the triangle.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 hinges on the 180°-sum (or its exterior-angle corollary) inside one triangle. A1 uses transversal/parallel rules without invoking a triangle interior sum; A3 requires the extra isosceles/equilateral side-equality step before the angle sum can be solved.

types.name: Find third angle given two
types.description: Stem names a triangle (often by vertices) and gives two of its interior angle measures (as numbers or simple expressions); ask for the third.
types.core_skill_demand: Compute 180 minus the sum of the two given angles to find the third.

characteristics: both given angles are explicit integer measures (no expressions)
answer is direct: 180 - g1 - g2
may be a right triangle so one angle is 90° and the task is 90 - g
may be figure-only stem where two interior angles are read from the diagram
easy.example_stem: In triangle XYZ, angle X = 23°, angle Y = 66°. Find angle Z.

characteristics: one of the angles is given as a symbolic expression like (k/2)° and student must solve for k
or the prompt asks which of several values 'could be' the third angle, requiring an inequality check (third > 0)
or the third angle is given by an expression of two equal angles (e.g., K and L both measure 48°, find J)
medium.example_stem: In triangle ABC, angle A = 54°, angle B = 90°, angle C = (k/2)°. Find k.

must_preserve: all three angles belong to one triangle
two of them are supplied (possibly with one of them being 90° if right triangle)
must_vary: vertex labels
whether one angle is given symbolically as a coefficient/parameter
whether the question asks for the angle value or a parameter k
common_bugs: agent forgets to halve or otherwise undo the (k/2) expression and reports the angle rather than k
agent inserts a non-triangle constraint (sum > 180)
agent makes the two given angles already sum to >180 in a clone, producing an impossible triangle

types.name: Exterior angle / linear-pair extension
types.description: A side of a triangle is extended past a vertex (forming a linear pair with one interior angle), or two sides are extended creating a vertical angle outside. Stem gives some adjacent angles and asks for an interior or exterior angle.
types.core_skill_demand: Use the exterior angle = sum of two remote interior angles, or convert exterior to interior via the linear pair (interior = 180 - exterior), then close with the 180° triangle sum.

characteristics: one side extended past one vertex; two interior angles supplied; answer is exterior angle = sum of two given interior angles, or a vertical-angle read off two extended sides
easy.example_stem: Two sides of a triangle are extended past the apex. Bottom angles are 70° and 50°. Find x at the apex.

characteristics: one side extended and the given quantities include an angle adjacent to the extension (e.g., PRS = 163°) plus another interior angle; student must use linear pair to recover one interior angle, then the 180° sum
medium.example_stem: QR extended to S; angle PQR = 132°, angle PRS = 163°. Find angle QPR.

must_preserve: an explicit extension of a side (or sides) of the triangle
exactly one unknown reachable via exterior-angle reasoning
must_vary: which vertex's side is extended
whether the given angle is interior or exterior
numeric values
common_bugs: agent treats the extended-side angle as an interior angle directly
agent misses that two extended sides produce a vertical angle equal to the apex angle
agent computes 180 - g when it should add (exterior-angle theorem)

types.name: Combined transversal + triangle (parallels inside/outside triangle)
types.description: Triangle interacts with parallel lines: a transversal of two parallel lines also forms a triangle, OR a segment parallel to one side of a triangle creates a smaller triangle, OR a parallel + extended-side situation requires both transversal and 180°-sum logic.
types.core_skill_demand: Use one parallel-line angle equality (corresponding or alternate-interior) to transfer an angle into the triangle, then apply the 180° sum to find the unknown.

characteristics: a transversal segment from one parallel line to another forms a triangle with one obvious leg parallel to a base; one or two angles given and the unknown is reachable in one alt-int + one triangle-sum step
easy.example_stem: Segment BC parallel to AD, line t passes through A and C, x = 22°. Find y.

characteristics: two segments meet at a point between parallels with two angles at the base specified; unknown is at the apex via two alt-int transfers + triangle sum (or polygon angle sum)
medium.example_stem: AE and BD parallel; angle BDC = 58°, angle ACE = 62°. Find angle CAE.

characteristics: right triangle with a segment parallel to one leg creating a similar smaller triangle and the question asks for an angle inside the smaller triangle
or a convex polygon with two non-adjacent parallel sides forcing a 540°-style angle sum
hard.example_stem: Right triangle XYZ with angle Y = 90°. PQ parallel to XY. Angle XZY = 63°. Find angle XPQ.

must_preserve: one explicit parallel statement that produces an angle transfer
a triangle (or polygon) interior-sum step required to finish
must_vary: which side is parallel to which
which angle is given vs requested
right-triangle vs general configuration
common_bugs: agent skips the parallel transfer and tries to compute the unknown directly from the triangle alone
agent applies the triangle 180°-sum to a quadrilateral (or vice versa)
agent confuses corresponding with alternate-interior, getting supplementary instead of equal angle

aspects.name: Isosceles / equilateral triangles
aspects.description: Exploit the side-equality of an isosceles triangle (base angles equal) or the all-equal property of an equilateral triangle to recover an unknown angle or to select a sufficient-information statement.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 always invokes a side-equality (AB = AC, or all three sides equal). A2's triangle-sum problems give all but one angle directly; A3 problems give fewer angles and require the side-equality to recover a base-angle equality before summing.

types.name: Find angle in isosceles triangle
types.description: Stem states (or shows) that two sides of a triangle are equal (or equivalently that two angles are equal) and gives one angle measure; ask for another angle or for x in an algebraic angle expression.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify which two angles are the base angles (opposite the equal sides), then use base-angles-equal plus the 180°-sum to find the unknown.

characteristics: isosceles flagged by equal-side notation (AB = AC) or by congruence of two angles in the figure
one given angle is one of the base angles or the apex; the unknown is a base angle
easy.example_stem: AB = AC and angle ABC = 67°. Find x.

characteristics: a real-world / dilation context wrapper that still reduces to isosceles
OR a 'NOT a possible measure of angle B' twist (multiple isosceles configurations to enumerate: apex vs base)
medium.example_stem: In triangle ABC, angle A = 50° and ABC is isosceles. Which is NOT a possible measure of angle B?

characteristics: two overlapping/intersecting triangles share a vertical-angle pair AND each have an equal-side property, so two isosceles arguments must be combined
or an isosceles triangle nested inside a configuration requiring exterior-angle and angle-sum steps
hard.example_stem: MQ and NR intersect at P; NP = QP, MP = PR. Find angle QMR.

must_preserve: an explicit side-equality (or its angle equivalent) so the base-angles-equal step is necessary
the 180°-sum closure step
must_vary: which pair of sides is equal
whether the given angle is apex or base
numeric values
common_bugs: agent assumes the given angle is a base angle when it is the apex (or vice versa) and produces the wrong base value
agent forgets the 'NOT possible' twist and reports a value that IS possible
agent treats two equal angles as the apex pair, violating triangle geometry

types.name: Sufficient information for equilateral / specific triangle
types.description: Stem gives a partial side-equality (e.g., AB = BC = 4680 mm) and asks which additional statement is sufficient to prove the triangle is equilateral (or otherwise specific).
types.core_skill_demand: Reason from the given equal-sides condition: equilateral requires all three sides equal OR all three angles equal OR a third condition that forces this (60° angle plus the existing equality).

characteristics: two of three sides already equal; pick the answer choice that supplies the third side equality or a 60° angle
easy.example_stem: AB = BC = 4680 mm. Which statement proves ABC is equilateral?

must_preserve: a partial equality already stated in the stem
answer choices include both sufficient and insufficient conditions
must_vary: which two sides are equal
which property (third side equal, 60° angle, two angles equal) is the correct sufficient one
common_bugs: agent picks a condition that is consistent but not sufficient (e.g., 'angle B is 70°')
agent picks a condition that re-states existing info
agent forgets that one 60° angle plus two equal sides forces equilateral

aspects.name: Triangle congruence
aspects.description: Use a stated congruence between two triangles (with explicit correspondence) to transfer angle (or side) measures from one triangle to the other, or determine what additional information is needed to establish congruence.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A4 hinges on a congruence statement (sides AND angles all equal, with a stated correspondence). A5 (similarity) preserves angles but scales sides; A4 preserves both.

types.name: Transfer angle via congruence
types.description: Two triangles are stated to be congruent with a specified vertex correspondence; one or two angles in the first triangle are given (often including a right angle); ask for an angle in the second.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the corresponding vertex in the second triangle, then either copy the matching angle directly (if it corresponds to a given one) or use the 180°-sum to derive it from the corresponding angles in the first.

characteristics: correspondence given as 'A↔D, B↔E, C↔F'
two angles given directly (e.g., A = 45°, F = 20°) so target angle is just 180 - (a + b)
or both triangles have a stated right angle (B = E = 90°) so target = 90 - a
easy.example_stem: Triangles ABC and DEF congruent; A↔D; B and E are right angles; A = 18°. Find F.

characteristics: same right-angle + correspondence setup but A's value is less standard (e.g., 27°, 69°), requiring 90 - a
vertex labels harder to map (E↔J, F↔K, G↔L)
medium.example_stem: Triangles ABC and DEF congruent; A↔D; B and E right angles; A = 69°. Find F.

must_preserve: explicit congruence statement with named correspondence
all the data needed to pin all three angles of one triangle (often via a right angle)
must_vary: which vertex is the right angle
numeric value of the given acute angle
which target vertex's angle is asked
common_bugs: agent maps the wrong corresponding vertex and reports the wrong angle
agent ignores the right-angle clue and tries to compute without enough data
agent simplifies away the correspondence and gives a generic third-angle answer

types.name: Determine sufficient information for congruence
types.description: Two triangles share some equal parts (e.g., two corresponding angles each); ask which additional piece of information would prove (or fail to prove) congruence.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that two equal corresponding angles do NOT establish congruence (only similarity); the additional piece must be a corresponding side (AAS / ASA) — not just any side that already pairs with equal angles in similarity.

characteristics: two angle equalities already given (so triangles are similar)
answer choices include sides that don't pin the scale plus the one side that does
hard.example_stem: Triangles ABC and DEF: angles B and E both 27°, C and F both 41°. Which extra fact proves congruence?

must_preserve: two angle equalities already supplied
answer must distinguish a corresponding-side fact from an unrelated-side fact
must_vary: which two angles are stated equal
which side becomes the correct discriminator
common_bugs: agent treats AA as sufficient for congruence
agent picks a side equality between non-corresponding sides
agent picks 'a third angle is equal' which is redundant

aspects.name: Triangle similarity
aspects.description: Use a stated or implied similarity between two triangles (or two figures) to relate angles (equal) and corresponding sides (proportional). Includes shadow / real-world proportional reasoning, dilation, side-ratio problems, and 'segment parallel to one side' configurations.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A5 always involves proportional sides with equal corresponding angles. A4 preserves both angles AND lengths (no scale factor). A2's parallel-line-inside-triangle problems that ask for angles are A2; A5's parallel-inside-triangle problems ask for lengths or use similarity explicitly.

types.name: Angle transfer via similarity
types.description: Two triangles (or figures) stated similar with a specified correspondence; ask for a corresponding angle.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that similar figures preserve angles, then transfer the named angle directly to its corresponding vertex.

characteristics: right-triangle similarity with vertex correspondence explicit; one acute angle given, find the corresponding acute angle in the other triangle
or a dilation with scale factor that does not affect the requested angle
easy.example_stem: Right triangles PQR and STU similar; P↔S; angle Q = 18°. Find angle S.

characteristics: scale factor or side-ratio is stated (e.g., k=3) but angle target requires the AA-correspondence reasoning + maybe 180°-sum
medium.example_stem: Triangles ABC and DEF similar; sides in ratio 1:3; angle BAC = 20°. Find angle EDF.

characteristics: correspondence is non-obvious or the question requires combining the similarity with another angle relationship (e.g., 2XY = RS distractor that does NOT change angles)
quadrilaterals rather than triangles, where the unstated fourth angle must be found via polygon sum
hard.example_stem: Similar quadrilaterals PQRS and WXYZ; angle S = 135°; PS = 45, WZ = 9. Find angle Z.

must_preserve: explicit similarity statement with correspondence
answer relies on angle preservation under similarity (not on scale)
must_vary: which angle is given vs asked
whether the figures are triangles or quadrilaterals
whether a misleading side-ratio is provided
common_bugs: agent scales the angle by the side ratio (e.g., reports 60° instead of 20° when k=3)
agent maps to the wrong vertex of the similar figure
agent omits the polygon-sum step when the requested angle is the fourth angle of a quadrilateral

types.name: Find side via proportional reasoning
types.description: Two similar triangles (or a real-world shadow scenario) with corresponding sides; one pair of corresponding sides is fully known (giving the scale factor) and another partial pair lets the student solve for a missing length.
types.core_skill_demand: Set up corresponding-side ratio AB/DE = BC/EF, substitute the three known lengths, and solve the proportion.

characteristics: real-world shadow / proportional context with three of four lengths given
scale factor is a clean integer or simple rational
easy.example_stem: Monument 555 ft tall casts a 300 ft shadow; tree casts a 16 ft shadow. Find tree height.

characteristics: abstract similar triangles with vertex correspondence; one pair of corresponding sides gives scale (e.g., AB=16, XY=?, factor 2); answer is direct multiplication or division
medium.example_stem: Triangle ABC similar to XYZ; XY = 2·AB; AB = 16. Find XY.

characteristics: a segment parallel to one side of a right or general triangle creates a similar sub-triangle; only an area or one extra length is given, forcing 2-step reasoning (use scale to find one length, then area or proportion to find another)
or a 'butterfly' / X-shape with two triangles sharing a vertex and stated parallel sides
or a coordinate-plane variant where two triangles share a vertex and corresponding vertices differ by a parameter k along one axis (asks for k that gives a stated side ratio)
hard.example_stem: Right triangle RST; LK parallel to RT, RT = 72, LK = 24, area RST = 792. Find KT.

must_preserve: explicit similarity or parallel-line-inside-triangle that forces similarity
exactly enough data so a single scale factor can be pinned
must_vary: which side pair carries the scale
real-world wrapper vs abstract
whether the requested side is in the larger or smaller triangle
common_bugs: agent inverts the ratio (uses larger/smaller when smaller/larger is needed)
agent maps non-corresponding sides into the proportion
agent simplifies away the area constraint and produces an under-determined problem in the hard variant

types.name: Similarity in overlapping / shared-vertex figures
types.description: Two triangles share a vertex (or intersect) with equal marked angles indicating similarity; one or more side lengths given; ask for a missing length or angle via the implied similar correspondence.
types.core_skill_demand: Identify the AA-similarity (often using vertical angles at the shared vertex plus a stated equal angle), establish the correspondence carefully (which vertex maps to which), then proportion the sides.

characteristics: two right triangles sharing a vertex with one matching acute angle
one full proportion to set up (one ratio of sides given, e.g., AE = 26·BD); answer is the unknown ratio
medium.example_stem: Triangle CAE similar to CBD; right angles at E and D; angle CBD = 57°; AE = 26·BD. Find a ratio.

characteristics: bow-tie / X configuration where line segments WZ and XY intersect at Q with angle-at-W equal to angle-at-Y, forcing AA similarity via vertical angles
four side lengths given, find a fifth via proportional + sometimes Pythagorean step
or an isosceles + similar nested config requiring identification of the correspondence
or a right-triangle-altitude configuration where altitude to the hypotenuse creates two similar sub-triangles
or two overlapping triangles on a common base line with intersecting cevians, requiring identifying which sub-triangles are similar
hard.example_stem: WZ and XY intersect at Q; angle W = angle Y = a°; YQ=21, WQ=70, WX=39. Find XY.

must_preserve: shared vertex or intersection that creates the AA similarity
exactly the data needed to set up one solvable proportion (no extras)
must_vary: which two triangles overlap (bow-tie vs nested)
which side is the unknown
whether the equal-angle cue is explicit or via parallel-line transfer
common_bugs: agent pairs non-corresponding sides because the figure is mirrored
agent forgets to add the shared segment when reporting the full length (XQ + QY)
agent treats the configuration as congruent rather than similar

types.name: Dilation / scale-factor reasoning
types.description: One figure is dilated by scale factor k to produce another; ask about a side length, or which value of k is admissible given a size constraint.
types.core_skill_demand: Apply k to multiply lengths (or divide if going from larger to smaller), and interpret k > 1 or k < 1 inequalities for admissibility questions.

characteristics: explicit scale-factor question (k > 1 for an enlargement) or 'each side of P'Q'R'S' is 3 times each side of PQRS' style asking for an angle (trick: angles preserved)
medium.example_stem: Equilateral T = k·S; sides of T greater than S. Which could be k?

must_preserve: a stated scale factor or 'each side is k times' phrasing
a quantity (length or angle) to report
must_vary: whether the answer is a length (scaled) or angle (unscaled)
direction of dilation (enlargement vs reduction)
common_bugs: agent scales the angle by k
agent reports k < 1 when an enlargement is required
agent confuses 'each side k times' with 'perimeter k times'

types.name: Sufficient info for similarity
types.description: Two triangles share two pairs of equal angles (and so are similar by AA), or share other partial info; stem asks which extra fact is needed to determine similarity (or congruence is asked among similarity setups).
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that AA already forces similarity; if the question is whether more is needed, the answer is 'none' or a side fact that doesn't reverse the conclusion. For sufficiency in non-AA configurations, identify whether SAS-similarity or SSS-similarity applies.

characteristics: two angle equalities already given so AA covers similarity; answer choice must reflect 'no additional info needed' or equivalent
medium.example_stem: DEF and RST: D=R=47°, E=S=97°. Which info is needed for similarity?

characteristics: one angle equality + one side ratio; answer must supply the second side ratio that makes SAS-similarity work, or a second angle for AA
hard.example_stem: LMN, RST: L = R = 60°, LN = 10, RT = 30. Which extra fact proves similarity?

must_preserve: enough partial info to make exactly one choice sufficient
distractors that are consistent but not sufficient
must_vary: whether AA, SAS-sim, or SSS-sim is the operative criterion
which side or angle is the discriminator
common_bugs: agent picks a redundant fact (third angle equality after two already match)
agent picks a side equality (which would suggest congruence) when only similarity is asked
agent misidentifies which sides are corresponding

aspects.name: Intersecting lines & angle relationships at a point
aspects.description: Single intersection (or three lines through one point) without parallelism: vertical angles equal, linear pairs sum to 180°, full turn sums to 360°. May include algebraic expressions for the angles or constraints on possible sums.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A6 involves only lines meeting at a single point (no parallel lines, no triangle sum). A1 requires parallelism; A2 requires a triangle.

types.name: Vertical / linear-pair angle measure
types.description: Two lines (or three) intersect at a point; one angle is given numerically; ask for a vertical-pair partner, the linear-pair partner, or — for three concurrent lines — the third angle so the half-turn sums to 180°.
types.core_skill_demand: Use vertical angles equal, linear pairs sum to 180°, or three angles on one side of a line sum to 180°.

characteristics: two lines crossing or three lines through a point
the unknown is directly the vertical or supplementary partner of a given numeric angle
easy.example_stem: Two lines meet; w = 136°. Find vertical-pair z.

characteristics: one of the angles is an expression in x (e.g., 9x - 140); identify which possible sums (of pairs of the four angles) cannot be produced
or geometry-grab-bag: figure-only stems with no readable text where the diagram supplies trapezoid / quadrilateral / right-triangle angle data
or area-constrained problems where the area inequality plus integer-side constraint pins a unique x
hard.example_stem: Two intersecting lines; one angle is (9x - 140)°. Which sum could NOT be a sum of two of the four angles?

must_preserve: intersection at a single point
no parallel-line invocation
must_vary: whether the answer is a specific value or a NOT-possible / could-be choice
whether one angle is symbolic
common_bugs: agent treats the two acute angles as supplementary instead of equal (and vice versa for obtuse pair)
agent solves for x but reports x instead of the requested angle sum
agent counts sums that cannot occur because the lines are not parallel (vs two lines vs two parallel lines distinction)

skill_wide_notes: Across the 84 originals: easy items lean heavily on direct angle-measure questions (find the partner angle / find the third angle / transfer a single angle through congruence or alternate-interior). Medium items add a single algebraic step (solve for k, then back-substitute) or a real-world wrapper. Hard items either (a) require chaining 2-3 angle-rule steps across multiple figures, (b) use similarity proportions in bow-tie / shared-vertex configurations, or (c) demand symbolic reasoning that an answer is independent of a parameter (the (9x - 140)°-style problems). Several hard originals (740bf79f, f88f27e5, 6a3fbec3, 933fee1a, eeb4143c, 81b664bc) have figure-only stems where the diagram, not the prose, carries the geometric data — clones must preserve that figure-dependence rather than verbalize all measurements.
